title: Al-Andalus
subtitle: 1031–1492 · Taifas, Berber Dynasties and the Last Kingdom of Granada

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From twenty-three taifas to the last sultanate of Granada

entries.label: The taifa kingdoms — fragmentation and patronage
entries.loc: Seville; Toledo; Zaragoza; Valencia; Granada (main taifas)
entries.hint: After the caliphate's collapse, al-Andalus fragments into 23 independent taifa kingdoms — each ruled by a former governor or military commander; the taifas pay tribute (parias) to the Christian kingdoms for protection; but they also become brilliant centres of poetry, music and philosophy.
entries.note: The taifa system: documented in Ibn Hayyan's Muqtabis (fragments) and Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib (13th c., drawing on earlier sources). The parias payments: documented in Christian royal archives (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo). Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal pp.130–160. The cultural flowering: best documented in the diwan (poetry collections) of taifa rulers, several of whom were themselves poets.

entries.label: Almoravid dynasty — Berber reform and reversal
entries.hint: The Almoravids arrive from Morocco at the taifas' invitation after Toledo falls (1085) — they defeat Alfonso VI at Sagrajas (1086), depose the taifa rulers, and temporarily reverse Christian advances; but their puritanical reform alienates the sophisticated Andalusian population.
entries.note: The Almoravid invitation: Ibn Idhari. The Battle of Sagrajas 23 Oct 1086: documented in both Arabic and Christian sources. The deposing of taifa rulers: documented in chronicles. Messier (2010), The Almoravids and the Meanings of Jihad: the standard modern study. The Almoravid architectural legacy in Marrakesh (UNESCO) and in Iberian buildings.

entries.label: Almohad Caliphate — the last North African empire in Iberia
entries.hint: The Almohads succeed the Almoravids — stricter Berber reformers who briefly exile Averroës (disgraced 1195, recalled to Marrakesh shortly before his death in 1198) and force Maimonides's family to flee Córdoba for Egypt; they build the Giralda tower in Seville (1184); their defeat at Las Navas (1212) begins the final collapse.
entries.note: The Giralda (1184): extant, Seville — the minaret built by the Almohads, now the cathedral's bell tower (UNESCO 1987). The exile of Averroës 1195: documented in Arabic sources. The defeat at Las Navas 1212: documented. Fromherz (2010), The Almohads: the standard modern study. The Almohad library programme: documented in their court records.

entries.label: Muhammad I founds the Nasrid Emirate of Granada
entries.hint: Muhammad I al-Ghalib founds the Nasrid dynasty in 1232 and begins the Alhambra fortress; he accepts tributary vassal status under Ferdinand III of Castile — a pragmatic bargain that keeps Granada independent for 250 more years; refugees from fallen Córdoba, Seville and Valencia enrich the new capital.
entries.note: Britannica: 'The first Naṣrid ruler, Muḥammad I al-Ghālib, a tributary vassal of the Christian king Ferdinand.' His accession 1238: documented in Arabic chronicles. The Alhambra's early construction: archaeology confirms 13th-c. foundations. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500: the standard modern study. The influx of refugees from reconquered cities: documented in Arabic and Christian sources.

entries.label: Muhammad V — the Alhambra at its peak
entries.hint: Muhammad V's two reigns see the completion of the Alhambra's greatest spaces — the Court of the Lions (1370s) and the Comares Hall; his court poet Ibn Zamrak inscribes poems on the walls of the palace; the Alhambra in its final form is his monument.
entries.note: Court of the Lions construction: dated by Ibn Zamrak's poems inscribed on the walls — the poem texts are the primary dating evidence. Ibn Zamrak's poetry on the Alhambra walls: primary source for the palace's meaning. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the standard art-historical study. Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra: the most comprehensive study of the inscriptions.

entries.label: Muhammad XII (Boabdil) — the last sultan
entries.hint: Muhammad XII (Boabdil) surrenders Granada to Ferdinand and Isabella on 2 January 1492 — handing over the keys of the Alhambra; legend says he wept at the last view of his palace (the 'Moor's last sigh' pass); the fall of Granada completes the Reconquista.
entries.note: The surrender 2 Jan 1492: documented in both Spanish and Arabic sources. The capitulations (terms of surrender): Archivo General de Simancas — guaranteeing religious freedom for Muslims (later violated). Washington Irving's Tales of the Alhambra (1832): romanticised but influential account. Harvey (1992) pp.280–310. The keys of the Alhambra: traditional account.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The collapse of Muslim Spain — taifas, crusades and the final siege

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa — already covered in spain-718-1230
entries.hint: The battle's significance is felt in this folio: after Las Navas, the Almohad empire collapses; within 40 years Córdoba (1236), Jaén (1246), Seville (1248) and Valencia (1238) fall; only Granada survives because it accepts Castilian vassalage.
entries.note: See spain-718-1230 folio for full documentation of the battle. The consequences here: Almohad collapse documented in Arabic sources. Ferdinand III's campaigns: documented in the Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X's chronicle, c.1270). The fall of Córdoba 29 Jun 1236: documented — the Great Mosque consecrated as a Christian church the same day. The fall of Seville 23 Nov 1248: documented.

entries.label: Granada as vassal — the 250-year survival strategy
entries.hint: The Nasrid Emirate survives for 250 years as a tributary vassal of Castile — paying annual tribute, providing military auxiliaries, and exploiting Christian internal conflicts; Muhammad I fought alongside Ferdinand III against Muslim opponents; survival through submission rather than resistance.
entries.note: Muhammad I's military service for Ferdinand III: documented in the Primera Crónica General. The annual tribute system: documented in Castilian royal accounts (AGS). Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500 ch.1. The exploitation of Christian civil wars (Pedro I vs Enrique II, 1366–1369): documented. Granada outlasts every other Muslim state in Iberia by adapting to vassal status.

entries.label: Mudéjar Revolt of 1264 — mass expulsion reshapes Andalusia
entries.loc: Seville; Jerez; Murcia (main centres of revolt)
entries.hint: Muslim populations (Mudéjars) in recently conquered Seville, Jerez and Murcia launch a major uprising backed by Muhammad I of Granada — the revolt is crushed by Castile with Aragonese help; the aftermath is the mass expulsion of Muslims from Castilian urban centres, transforming Andalusia's cities into purely Christian-settled areas and creating the hostile 'La Frontera' zone.
entries.note: The revolt: Crónica de Alfonso X (primary). Muhammad I of Granada's role: documented in both Castilian chronicles and Arabic sources. The expulsions: documented in subsequent repopulation charters (AGS). Burns (1973), Islam Under the Crusaders: the standard study of Mudéjar experience in the 13th century. The long-term demographic consequence: Andalusian cities lose their Muslim populations — documented in urban archaeology and subsequent census records.

entries.label: Battle of Río Salado — the end of North African intervention
entries.hint: Alfonso XI of Castile and Alfonso IV of Portugal crush the combined armies of the Marinid Sultan of Morocco and the Nasrid Sultan of Granada at Río Salado — this catastrophic defeat permanently ends North African military intervention in the Iberian Peninsula, leaving Granada completely isolated and entirely dependent on its defensive geography and tributary diplomacy.
entries.note: The battle 30 Oct 1340: documented in the Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary Castilian source). The Marinid forces: Ibn Khaldun, Muqaddima: contextualises the Marinid dynasty's involvement. The battle's long-term consequence: no further North African force crosses to Iberia after 1340 — documented through the absence of subsequent interventions. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: contextualises Río Salado in the broader narrative.

entries.label: The fall of Granada — the last Reconquista campaign
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella's systematic campaign against Granada (1481–1492) takes the remaining towns one by one — Alhama (1482), Ronda (1485), Málaga (1487), Baza (1489); Granada surrenders 2 January 1492 under capitulation terms that are violated within a decade.
entries.note: The capitulations 2 Jan 1492: AGS Patronato Real — primary text. The systematic campaign: documented in the Primera Crónica de Fernando e Isabel (published Pulgar 1490). Prescott (1838), History of the Reign of Ferdinand and Isabella: the classic account. Harvey (1992) pp.280–315. The violation of the capitulations (forced conversions begin c.1502): documented in royal decrees.

entries.label: Nasrid civil wars — faction warfare enables the final conquest
entries.hint: The final Castilian campaign exploits Granada's internal collapse — the rival noble clans (Abencerrajes and Zenetes) wage factional war; Muley Hacén is deposed by his son Boabdil (Muhammad XII); civil war then erupts between Boabdil and his uncle El Zagal (Muhammad XIII); Ferdinand and Isabella pick off isolated towns as the kingdom tears itself apart.
entries.note: The Nasrid dynastic conflict: documented in Arabic sources and in the Castilian court chronicles. The Abencerrajes and Zenetes: their rivalry documented in Arabic sources and in Washington Irving's (romanticised) Tales of the Alhambra. The deposition of Muley Hacén: documented. Boabdil's capture by Ferdinand at Lucena 1483: documented — he was released in exchange for a treaty recognising Castilian suzerainty. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain pp.265–280: the standard account using Arabic and Castilian sources.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Nasrid administration and the capitulations of Granada

entries.label: Taifa courts as cultural patronage states
entries.hint: The taifa rulers compensate for political weakness with cultural patronage — al-Mu'tamid of Seville is himself a celebrated poet, al-Muqtadir of Zaragoza builds the Aljafería palace (extant, UNESCO), the Banu Hud patronise music and science; the taifa courts are the peak of Andalusian cultural production.
entries.note: Al-Mu'tamid's poetry: diwan preserved in multiple manuscripts; Nykl (1946), Hispano-Arabic Poetry. The Aljafería palace (c.1065): extant, Zaragoza (UNESCO). Al-Muqtadir's court: documented in Ibn Idhari. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate, ch.4–5. The Aljafería is the best-preserved taifa palace — its archaeology provides the primary material evidence for taifa court culture.

entries.label: Capitulations of Granada — the broken promise
entries.hint: The capitulations guaranteeing Muslims religious freedom, property rights and continued use of mosques are signed before the surrender — within a decade Archbishop Cisneros forces mass baptisms; the capitulations are formally the founding document of Spain's broken covenant with its Muslim population.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Capitulations text: AGS Patronato Real, leg.11, doc.27. Translated in Constable (1997), Medieval Iberia. The violation: Cisneros's forced conversions from 1499 — documented in his biographers and in AGS. Harvey (1990), Islamic Spain: the standard study of the Mudéjar and Morisco experience. The capitulations and their violation are the clearest documented case of Spain's treatment of its Muslim subjects.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Almohad orthodoxy, Sufi mysticism and Ibn Arabi

entries.label: Ibn Arabi — the greatest Sufi mystic of Islam
entries.hint: Muhyi al-Din ibn Arabi is born in Murcia, educated in Seville, and becomes the most influential mystic philosopher in Islamic history — his Fusus al-Hikam (Bezels of Wisdom) and Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya (Meccan Revelations) synthesise Sufi thought; he is the 'Greatest Sheikh' (al-Shaykh al-Akbar).
entries.note: Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya (Meccan Revelations): multiple manuscripts; Chittick (1989), The Sufi Path of Knowledge: the standard English study. Fusus al-Hikam: multiple manuscripts; Austin (1980), Bezels of Wisdom. Ibn Arabi's Andalusian origins: documented in his autobiography Ruh al-quds. Austin (1971), Sufis of Andalusia: documents his Andalusian teachers. Ibn Arabi spent his formative years in al-Andalus before leaving for Mecca in 1200.

entries.label: The Alhambra — a palace of Quranic inscriptions
entries.hint: The Alhambra's walls are covered with over 10,000 Arabic inscriptions — Quranic verses, poems, and the repeated phrase 'There is no victor but God' (the Nasrid motto); the inscriptions make the palace simultaneously an architecture of power and a theological statement.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). The inscriptions corpus: Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra — the comprehensive catalogue of all inscriptions. The Ibn Zamrak poems (Muhammad V's court poet): primary source for the palace's self-understanding. The phrase 'Wa la ghalib illa Allah' (No victor but God): appears hundreds of times — the Nasrid motto. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the foundational art-historical analysis.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: The Alhambra, taifa poetry and the last flowering of Andalusian culture

entries.label: Andalusian musical tradition — the nuba system
entries.hint: The Andalusian musical tradition (al-musiqa al-andalusiyya) develops in the taifa and Nasrid periods — the nuba (suite) system organises compositions by mode and rhythm; after 1492 the tradition is carried to Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia by exiles where it still survives as living performance practice.
entries.note: The nuba system: documented in Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddima (14th c.) — the most detailed medieval account. Farmer (1929), A History of Arabian Music: the standard older study. The survival in North Africa: documented in the contemporary practice of the Moroccan Andalusian orchestra. The connection to Ziryab's 9th-c. foundations: traced through Arabic musical theory treatises. Reynolds (2008), The Cambridge Companion to Medieval Music ch.12.

entries.label: Al-Mu'tamid of Seville — the poet-king
entries.hint: Al-Mu'tamid ibn Abbad, ruler of Seville (1069–1091), is the greatest poet of the taifa era — he writes with his court poet Ibn Ammar, rules a brilliant court, and ends his life in exile in Morocco after inviting the Almoravids who then depose him; his elegy from exile is one of the most moving Arabic poems.
entries.note: Al-Mu'tamid's diwan: manuscripts preserved in multiple Arabic libraries; Nykl (1946), Hispano-Arabic Poetry: selections translated. His elegy from Moroccan exile: widely anthologised. The story of his relationship with Ibn Ammar: documented in biographical sources. He died in Aghmat, Morocco, 1095 — his tomb became a pilgrimage site. García Gómez (1944), Poemas arabigoandaluces: the standard Spanish anthology.

entries.label: The Alhambra — the palace of water, light and inscription
entries.hint: The Alhambra is the most complete surviving medieval Islamic palace complex in the world — the Comares Hall (throne room), Court of the Lions (begun 1370s), Generalife gardens; its system of water channels, stucco decoration and calligraphic inscriptions are the primary evidence for Nasrid court culture.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). Ongoing ALHAMAT archaeological project (Cambridge 2021–2022): documents building phases using radiocarbon dating — published Antiquity 2025. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the foundational study. The Court of the Lions: dated by Ibn Zamrak's inscribed poems. Dodds (1992), Architecture and Ideology in Early Medieval Spain: contextualises within Iberian tradition.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Averroës, Maimonides and the philosophical Golden Age

entries.label: Averroës (Ibn Rushd) — Aristotle reaches Europe through Córdoba
entries.hint: Ibn Rushd (Averroës) writes commentaries on virtually all of Aristotle's works between 1169 and 1195 — translated into Latin and Hebrew, they make Aristotle accessible to medieval Europe for the first time; his medical encyclopaedia Colliget becomes a European textbook; he is exiled by the Almohads in 1195.
entries.note: Averroës's commentaries (1169–1195): Arabic originals and Latin translations. Major Latin manuscripts: BnF and British Library. Colliget (Al-Kulliyat fi al-Tibb): translated as Colliget — European university textbook for centuries. The exile of 1195: documented in Arabic sources. Taylor & López-Farjeat (2016), The Routledge Companion to Islamic Philosophy: the standard survey. The 'Commentator' sobriquet (Dante calls him this): documented in medieval European sources.

entries.label: Maimonides (Ibn Maymun) — Guide for the Perplexed
entries.hint: Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides) is born in Córdoba in 1138 — forced to flee the Almohad persecution, he eventually settles in Egypt; his Guide for the Perplexed (c.1190) reconciles Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology and becomes the most influential medieval Jewish philosophical text.
entries.note: Guide for the Perplexed (Dalalat al-Ha'irin, c.1190): Arabic original; Hebrew translation by Samuel ibn Tibbon (1204) — BnF. Maimonides's birth in Córdoba 1138: documented. The Almohad persecution forcing his family to flee: documented in his own writings. Pines (1963), Guide of the Perplexed (Chicago): the standard English translation. His Mishneh Torah (Hebrew law code, 1180): the other major work.

entries.label: Ibn al-Khatib — vizier, historian and voice of the Alhambra
entries.loc: Granada (court of Yusuf I and Muhammad V); Fez (exile and death)
entries.hint: Lisan al-Din ibn al-Khatib is the dominant intellectual figure of Nasrid Granada — chief minister (vizier) under Yusuf I and Muhammad V, author of the Ihata (the definitive history of Granada), and poet whose verses are inscribed on the walls of the Alhambra's Court of the Myrtles; his dramatic fall from favour, exile and execution in Fez embodies late Nasrid political volatility.
entries.note: The Ihata fi akhbar Gharnata (History of Granada): multiple Arabic manuscripts — the definitive history of the Nasrid kingdom. Ibn al-Khatib's poems in the Alhambra: identified and documented in Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra — his verses appear in the Court of the Myrtles. His execution in Fez 1374: documented in Arabic sources — strangled in prison on charges of heresy brought by enemies in Granada. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500 ch.8: the standard English account.

entries.label: Ibn Khaldun — the Muqaddima and the philosophy of history
entries.hint: Ibn Khaldun visits Granada briefly but his Muqaddima (Prolegomena, 1377) — the introduction to his universal history — is deeply shaped by his Andalusian cultural inheritance; it is the first work to propose a cyclical theory of history based on social cohesion (asabiyya) and is sometimes called the first work of sociology.
entries.note: Muqaddima (1377): Arabic manuscripts in multiple libraries; Rosenthal translation (Princeton 1967), 3 vols. Ibn Khaldun's visit to Granada and meeting with Pedro I of Castile: documented in his Autobiography (Ta'rif). Lacoste (1984), Ibn Khaldun: the Birth of History and the Past of the Third World: the standard modern study. His Andalusian connections: documented in the autobiography.

entries.label: Ibn Bajja (Avempace) — the regime of the solitary
entries.loc: Zaragoza (born; court of Banu Hud); Seville; Fez (died)
entries.hint: Ibn Bajja (Avempace) argues in Tadbir al-Mutawahhid (The Regime of the Solitary) that the rational thinker must intellectually isolate from a corrupt society to achieve union with the Active Intellect — the foundational text for both Averroës and Maimonides; his political philosophy directly shapes the tradition of Andalusi rationalism.
entries.note: Tadbir al-Mutawahhid: Arabic manuscript — partial critical edition Asín Palacios (1946), El régimen del solitario. The Active Intellect theory: Ibn Bajja's adaptation of al-Farabi documented in the text. Genequand (2001), 'Ibn Bajja et la science divine' in Mélanges de l'Université Saint-Joseph: the standard modern study. The connection to Averroës: explicitly acknowledged in Ibn Rushd's own commentary on De Anima. The connection to Maimonides: documented in Guide for the Perplexed I.74 — he cites Ibn Bajja.

entries.label: Ibn Tufail — Hayy ibn Yaqzan, the first philosophical novel
entries.hint: Ibn Tufail's Hayy ibn Yaqzan (The Living, Son of the Awake, c.1165) is the first philosophical novel in history — a feral child on a desert island discovers through pure reason and observation alone the supreme truths of nature, metaphysics and God; it directly influences John Locke's tabula rasa and serves as a major precursor to the European Enlightenment.
entries.note: Hayy ibn Yaqzan (c.1165): Arabic manuscripts; first Latin translation Pococke (1671) as Philosophus Autodidactus — the vehicle of its European influence. English translation Goodman (2009), Ibn Tufayl's Hayy Ibn Yaqzan. The influence on Locke: documented in Locke's library catalogue (Christ Church Oxford) — he owned a copy of the 1671 Latin translation. Russell (1994), 'The Impact of the Philosophus Autodidactus': the standard study of its European impact. Ibn Tufail's role at the Almohad court: documented in biographic dictionaries.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Trade, silk and the economy of the last Islamic state

entries.label: Granada's silk economy — the maristán and the silk market
entries.hint: The Nasrid Emirate of Granada sustains its independence partly through a sophisticated silk economy — the Vega de Granada produces raw silk exported across the Mediterranean; the Alcaicería (silk market) and the maristán (hospital funded by silk revenues) document a complex urban economy.
entries.note: The Alcaicería: documented in Arabic sources and urban archaeology. The maristán (hospital, c.1367): foundation inscription survives — it records that Muhammad V funded it from silk revenues (Puerta Vílchez 2010). Constable (1994), Trade and Traders in Muslim Spain: the standard economic study. The silk export documentation: Genoese merchant records (Archivio di Stato, Genoa) document Granadan silk exports.

entries.label: Black Death in al-Andalus — Ibn Khatima's treatise
entries.hint: The Black Death reaches al-Andalus via Almería in 1348 — the Almerian physician Ibn Khatima writes the first Arabic treatise specifically on plague, proposing a theory of contagion through tiny bodies entering the body; his Andalusian colleague Ibn al-Khatib writes a second treatise independently reaching similar conclusions.
entries.note: Ibn Khatima, Tahqiq al-naba' an amr al-waba' (1349): Arabic manuscript — Biblioteca Nacional de España. Ibn al-Khatib, Muqni'at al-sa'il (c.1362): also Arabic manuscript. Both propose germ-like theories of contagion 500 years before Pasteur. Dols (1977), The Black Death in the Middle East: the standard study. The Tàrrega pogrom 1348 (see Lives): documented genetically (MDPI 2026 study).

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Toledo translations, diplomatic exchange and the Andalusian diaspora

entries.label: The Andalusian diaspora — knowledge carries west and east
entries.hint: Each Christian conquest sends waves of Andalusian scholars and artisans into the Islamic world — after 1492, the entire surviving Muslim community of Spain (Mudejars) is expelled or forced to convert; they carry Andalusian architecture, music, cuisine and scholarship to Morocco and the Ottoman Empire.
entries.note: The Nasrid refugees after 1492: Harvey (1990), Islamic Spain — the standard study. The Andalusian architectural influence in Morocco: the Bou Inania madrasa in Fez (1351) uses craftsmen from Granada — documented. The survival of Andalusian music in North Africa: Reynolds (2008). The genetic evidence for the diaspora: recent DNA studies document Sephardic and Morisco genetic clusters in Morocco and Turkey.

entries.label: Toledo school — Averroës translated into Latin
entries.hint: After Toledo's reconquest (1085) the translation school reaches its peak in the 12th–13th century — Michael Scot translates Averroës's Aristotle commentaries into Latin (c.1217, working in Toledo then Italy), Herman the German continues the work; these translations make Andalusian scholarship the foundation of European university curricula.
entries.note: Michael Scot's translations of Averroës (c.1217 onwards): Latin manuscripts in BnF and British Library — he translated the major commentaries on Aristotle while working in Toledo and Sicily. Herman the German (Hermannus Alemannus, mid-13th c.): further translations. Gerard of Cremona (d.1187) did not translate Averroës — chronologically impossible as Averroës's major commentaries (1169–1195) postdate Cremona's death. Burnett (2001), 'The Coherence of the Arabic-Latin Translation Program in Toledo'. The impact on European universities: Averroës becomes required reading at Paris by the 1240s.

entries.label: Battle of the Strait — geopolitical contest over Gibraltar
entries.hint: The Strait of Gibraltar is the most heavily contested choke-point in the medieval West — Castile, Aragon, Granada and the Marinids of Morocco fight over it for decades; controlling the Strait determines whether North African forces can reinforce Iberia; the final closure to North African intervention is sealed at Río Salado (1340) and documented in the capture of Algeciras (1344).
entries.note: The 'Battle of the Strait' concept: O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study using Castilian and Moroccan sources. The Marinid crossings: documented in both Arabic and Castilian chronicles. The strategic importance of Tarifa: its capture by Castile (1292) and the defence by Guzmán el Bueno (documented) — a turning point. The 1340 Río Salado and 1344 capture of Algeciras: Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary). The Strait's closure: the geopolitical endpoint of North African intervention in Europe.

entries.label: 1492 — three events in one year
entries.hint: 1492 is the hinge year of Spanish history: Granada falls (2 January), the Jews are expelled (31 March Alhambra Decree), and Columbus departs for the Americas (3 August) — all three authorised by the same monarchs from the same palace; the end of medieval Spain and the beginning of the global empire.
entries.note: The Alhambra Decree (31 Mar 1492): AGS Patronato Real — expelling all unbaptised Jews. Columbus's departure: documented in his journal (copy: BnF). The co-incidence of these three events in one year from the Alhambra: the central argument of Colon (2004), 1492: The Poetics of Diaspora. The three events' connection: explicitly noted by contemporaries including Peter Martyr d'Anghiera in letters dated that year.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: The 'lady of the Alhambra' — Nasrid women in inscriptions
entries.hint: The Alhambra's inscriptions include dedications to royal women — the Mexuar hall carries inscriptions referencing the sultan's mother; Nasrid women are documented as patrons of religious foundations; the Generalife gardens are associated with female royal use; the evidence is fragmented but present.
entries.note: Alhambra inscriptions mentioning royal women: Puerta Vílchez (2010) catalogues them. The patronage of religious foundations by Nasrid women: Harvey (1992). The Generalife: its association with female royal quarters documented in Arabic literary sources. The Moroccan connection — Nasrid women's letters preserved in Moroccan archives (unpublished): noted in Harvey (1992) as an under-researched area.

events.l: Mudéjar Revolt — mass expulsion of Muslims
Battle of Río Salado — North Africa halted
Ibn Arabi born — greatest Sufi mystic
Ibn al-Khatib — vizier and historian of Granada
Battle of the Strait — Gibraltar contested
